summary: Rule chunk from Gamemastering Paranoia page 101, chunk 2.

mechanics.raw_text:
For example, fantasy literature and roleplay- ing games often center around some impor- lant quest to find a princess or grab some fabulous loot or achieve peace in our time or something. And space opera literature and roleplaying games often center around over- throwing evil empires or slaughtering nasty aliens oF grabbing fabulous loot or searching, for ancient astronauts or something, In Paranoia there is always the mission, but rho one (except The Computer) ever takes it all that seriously. And for good reason. Most missions are suicide festivals, wild bot chases, programming errors, or conspiracies so in: tricate and obscure that even the GM doesn’t, understand them, Often the PCs aren’t sup- posed to be able to achieve the mission’s ob: Jectives. Often they can’t even understand the ‘mission’s objectives, much less achieve them. But that’s Paranoia for you. The central nar rative isn’t all that central in Paranoia. Often it’s just an excuse to get the characters ous in to a hallway where they can shoot at each ‘other while they are run aver by transbots and, incinerated by malfunctioning suit heaters and sniped at by traitors and cheerully bullied by Their Friend The Computer and all its litle playmates, 
The incidental horrors that befall the characters are the real meat of a Paranoia adventure, 
So the players have finally completed the mission. Or botched it beyond any hope of success, Or you're tired and want to break things up. Okay; the Troubleshooters are sum. moned to a Debriefing "ere they account for their performance to their superiors (usually the briefing officer and any other interested parties) and The Com puter.
